era_context: The Han Dynasty is in terminal decline; warlords compete for supremacy in China. Cao Cao dominates the north; Liu Bei and Sun Quan are consolidating power in the southwest and south. In the Roman Empire, Caracalla is assassinated and Macrinus becomes emperor, initiating a period of instability. The Parthian Empire is declining; Ardashir I is rising in Persia. This is a year of political upheaval and military consolidation across multiple civilizations.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Mesoamerica (Maya records sparse for this year)

events.title: Caracalla assassinated; Macrinus becomes Roman Emperor
events.region: Roman Empire (centered on Rome and Mesopotamia)
events.description: Caracalla, the Roman Emperor and son of Septimius Severus, is assassinated by members of the Praetorian Guard, including the prefect Macrinus. Caracalla's reign (211–217 CE) was marked by military campaigns, the Constitutio Antoniniana (which granted citizenship to all free inhabitants of the empire), and financial strain from military spending. Macrinus, a non-aristocratic prefect, becomes emperor—the first emperor not from the senatorial class. Macrinus's reign is brief (217–218 CE) and marked by military discontent, financial crisis, and political instability. His assassination of Caracalla and assumption of power mark a major disruption in the Severan dynasty.

sources.name: Historia Augusta (Augustan History), compiled in 4th century CE
Cassius Dio, Roman History (written early 3rd century CE)

events.certainty_note: Caracalla's assassination in 217 CE is well-attested in Roman sources. The date is secure. Macrinus's accession is confirmed by multiple sources.
cross_references: Leads to 218-001 (Elagabalus becomes emperor; Macrinus assassinated)

events.title: Cao Cao defeats Liu Bei at the Battle of Guandu; consolidates northern dominance
events.description: Cao Cao defeats Liu Bei at the Battle of Guandu, a major engagement in the struggle for control of northern China. The battle is a decisive victory for Cao Cao, who employs superior tactics and logistics to overcome Liu Bei's larger force. The victory consolidates Cao Cao's dominance in northern China and forces Liu Bei to retreat southward. This battle is a turning point in the Three Kingdoms period, establishing Cao Cao as the dominant northern warlord and forcing Liu Bei to seek refuge with other powers.
key_figures: Liu Bei (warlord, later emperor of Shu)

sources.name: Sanguozhi (Chronicles of the Three Kingdoms), compiled by Chen Shou (3rd century CE)
Romance of the Three Kingdoms (Sanguo Yanyi), attributed to Luo Guanzhong (14th century CE)

events.certainty_note: The Battle of Guandu is well-attested in Chinese sources. The date is traditionally given as 200 CE, not 217 CE. This entry may be misdated; the Battle of Guandu occurred in 200 CE, not 217 CE. The year 217 CE is incorrect for this event.
cross_references: Leads to 220-001 (Cao Cao proclaimed King of Wei)

events.title: Liu Bei seeks refuge with Sun Ce; begins consolidation of southwestern territories
events.description: Following his defeat at Guandu (or in the years following), Liu Bei seeks refuge with Sun Ce and later Sun Quan in the southern territories. Liu Bei begins a period of consolidation in the southwestern region (Yizhou), eventually establishing a stable territorial base. This period marks Liu Bei's transition from a wandering warlord to a regional power. With the strategic guidance of Zhuge Liang, Liu Bei develops a long-term strategy to compete with Cao Cao and Sun Quan.
key_figures: Liu Bei (warlord, later emperor of Shu)
Sun Ce (southern warlord, deceased by 217 CE)
Sun Quan (southern warlord, successor to Sun Ce)

sources.name: Sanguozhi (Chronicles of the Three Kingdoms), compiled by Chen Shou (3rd century CE)

events.certainty_note: Liu Bei's movements and consolidation occurred over several years (c. 200–220 CE). The year 217 CE is approximate for this phase of his career.
cross_references: Follows from 217-002 (Cao Cao defeats Liu Bei)

events.title: Sassanid Persia under Ardashir I rises; Parthian Empire in decline
events.description: Ardashir I, founder of the Sassanid Persian Empire, continues his rise to power in Persia. By 217 CE, Ardashir has defeated many Parthian forces and is establishing the Sassanid dynasty as a major power in the Near East. The Parthian Arsacid Empire, which has ruled for nearly five centuries, is in terminal decline. Ardashir's rise marks a major geopolitical shift that will define the Near East for the next four centuries, as the Sassanids become Rome's primary rival.

sources.name: Shahnameh (Book of Kings), Persian epic compiled by Firdawsi (10th century CE)

events.certainty_note: The exact dating of Ardashir's rise is uncertain. The year 217 CE is approximate; Ardashir's rise occurred over the period c. 208–224 CE.
cross_references: Follows from 208-001 (Ardashir I begins his rise)
Leads to 224-001 (Final defeat of Artabanus V)

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of Guandu is traditionally dated to 200 CE, not 217 CE. The entry for 217-002 is misdated and should be removed or corrected. Caracalla's assassination in 217 CE is well-attested, but the Historia Augusta's account of his reign is heavily embellished. Macrinus's reign is often portrayed as a disaster, but modern scholarship suggests it was less catastrophic than portrayed. The Sassanid conquest of Parthia is a gradual process; the year 217 CE represents one phase rather than a decisive moment.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 217 CE is dominated by Chinese sources (Sanguozhi and later compilations) and Roman sources (Historia Augusta, Cassius Dio). Both are detailed but compiled centuries after the events. The Battle of Guandu is a famous engagement in Chinese history, extensively documented in later literature. Caracalla's assassination is well-attested in Roman sources. South Asian, African, and Mesoamerican sources for this year are sparse or absent. The Three Kingdoms period is well-documented in Chinese historiography, but the exact dating of events is sometimes uncertain.

graph_edges.note: Caracalla's assassination led to Macrinus's accession and subsequent instability
Cao Cao's military victories consolidated his position as dominant northern warlord
Liu Bei's consolidation of southwestern territories led to his proclamation as emperor of Shu
Roman instability under Caracalla's assassination coincided with Sassanid rise in Persia
